content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

October 25, 1976


Robert W. Parsons
Superintendent
College School District
Post Office Box 188
Santa Ynez, California 93460

Dear Mr. Parsons:

     Senator Rains has provided our agency with a copy of the
resolution adopted by the board of your school district on
September 20, 1976. Essentially, the resolution asks the
Legislature to amend the Political Reform Act of 1974 to ex-
empt elected school board members from the financial dis-
closure provisions of Government Code Sections 87100, et seq.

     It is understandable that unpaid school board members
believe it is an imposition to be compelled to disclose certain
of their private financial interests. However, under existing
law, school districts are governmental agencies; members of
boards of education as well as certain administrative personnel
within the school districts "make or participate in the making
of governmental decisions" within the meaning of the Political
Reform Act; and many of their decisions affect private financial
interests. Under these circumstances, the Act requires that
school districts adopt Conflict of Interest Codes applicable
to members and officials who are capable of affecting such
financial interests. Only those financial interests which
could be affected by the filer's official actions must be
disclosed, and it is the school district which has the first
and primary responsibility to identify these potential conflicts
of interest and provide for their disclosure. Hopefully,
individuals who object to overly broad disclosure will take an
active role in the design of each code's disclosure require-
ments, so that intrusion of personal privacy will be kept at a
minimum consistent with the objective of preventing or disclos-
ing conflicts of interest.

     The Fair Political Practices Commission is required to
and has provided technical assistance to state and local agencies
in drafting codes. In particular, we have urged agencies to
avoid the easy path of imposing disclosure requirements with a
broad brush but rather to describe with as much precision as
possible the particular types of financial interests which
could be affected by official decisions and which therefore
must be disclosed.


TECHNICAL ASSISTANCE/ADMINISTRATION (916) 322-5660 • EXECUTIVE/LEGAL 322-5901 • ENFORCEMENT/CONFLICT OF INTEREST 322-6441

I can see this is a mostly blank page with what appears to be two small moon or crescent shapes near the top, and possibly some very faint marks or specks scattered on the page. However, there is no clearly legible text content to transcribe from this document image.

Robert W. Parsons
October 25, 1976
Page Two


The Political Reform Act was, of course, passed as an
initiative. Section 81012(a) of the Act provides that the Act
may be amended to further its purposes. Among those purposes
are the following:

     Assets and income of public officials which
     may be materially affected by their official
     actions should be disclosed and in appropriate
     circumstances the officials should be dis-
     qualified from acting in order that conflicts
     of interest may be avoided.

                              Gov. Code § 81002(g).

Given the circumstances, it is doubtful that the exemption of
school board members from the requirements of the Act could be
construed to be in furtherance of the purposes of the Act.

     Even assuming, however, that your proposal could be adopted
by the Legislature, and fully recognizing the right of school
board members to express their views, I firmly disagree with
the proposal. School boards make decisions of great consequence
to their communities. Like other public officials, school
board members serve in a position of public trust, and the
people have the right to be assured that their decisions will
be made free from bias caused by their own financial interests.
Disclosure of any financial interests which could foreseeably
be affected by their official actions is a reasonable and
effective means of preventing conflicts of interest. In recent
years the California Supreme Court and the courts of many
other states have ruled that such disclosure requirements are
valid and that any consequent intrusion of the privacy of the
official is justified by the important public interest that is
served.

     I hope that this information is helpful. If you have
further questions, please call Mr. Delbert Spurlock, Chief of
our Conflicts of Interest Division, at (916) 322-6444.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        [signature]

                                        Michael Bennett
                                        Executive Director

MB:plh

cc: Senator Omer Rains

[The image appears to be almost entirely blank or white with only very faint marks visible at the top and a small mark in the bottom right corner. The content is not legible enough to transcribe with confidence.]

MEMBERS
OMER L. RAINS
CHAIRMAN
M. L. RICHARDSON
VICE CHAIRMAN
DAVID CARPENTER
ALEX P. GARCIA
BILL GREENE
ARLEN GREGORIO
NATE HOLDEN

COMMITTEE ADDRESS
ROOM 5090, STATE CAPITOL
SACRAMENTO, CA 95614
(916) 445-2631

California Legislature

JAMES A. BROWNING, JR.
CONSULTANT

SUSAN C. TONINI
COMMITTEE SECRETARY

SENATE COMMITTEE ON
ELECTIONS AND REAPPORTIONMENT

OMER L. RAINS
CHAIRMAN

October 21, 1976

Robert W. Parsons
Superintendent
College School District
Post Office Box 188
Santa Ynez, California 93460

Dear Mr. Parsons:

Thank you for sending me a copy of your school board's resolution,
expressing the view that local school board members should be
excluded from the conflict of interest provisions of the Political
Reform Act.

The Act, at Section 87300, requires agencies to adopt and
promulgate a conflict of interest code. "Agency" at Section 82003
includes "local government agency". "Local government agency"
includes "school district". Section 82041

I sympathize with your view that these provisions are burdensome
on persons serving, in many cases, without any renumeration at all.
I assume, however, the drafters of the initiative (and perhaps the
public in voting for the initiative) perceived the real threat of
conflicts of interest in awarding contracts in the school district
as well as any other legislative or administrative body. In fact,
it is arguable, that since such a large percentage of a school
board's business is awarding contracts, such boards should be
included.

In any event, since the Political Reform Act was an initiative
measure, it can only be amended by a vote of the people, except an
amendment "to further its purposes" could be passed by a two-thirds
vote of each house of the Legislature. A question arises as to
whether such an amendment would be construed to "further its purposes".
My staff is reviewing your suggestion and an appropriate remedy.

[illegible]

Robert W. Parsons                    2                    October 21, 1976


For the views of the Fair Political Practices Commission, I am
forwarding this letter to Robert Stern, general counsel.

Very truly yours,


OMER L. RAINS
OLR/jak
cc:  Robert Stern, General Counsel, Fair Political Practices
     Commission, 925 J Street, Suite 522, Sacramento, California
     95814

I see a mostly blank white page with what appears to be three small circular shapes near the top of the image and some very faint dots or marks in the upper right corner. The rest of the page appears to be empty or contains content that is not legible in this image.

ROBERT W. PARSONS
DISTRICT SUPERINTENDENT

COLLEGE SCHOOL DISTRICT
SANTA YNEZ, CALIFORNIA 93460
TELEPHONE: 688-5196

RESOLUTION # 1-76

CONFLICT OF INTEREST

WHEREAS, public school boards are required by public law (Government
Code Sections 87100 et seq.) to prepare and adopt a district
Conflict of Interest Code, and

WHEREAS, board members of said school boards are required by law
to file a disclosure statement disclosing their interest
and those of their immediate families in investments, real
property, and income, and

WHEREAS, these disclosure statements are by law made available for
public scrutiny, and

WHEREAS, other sections of the Government Code (Section 1090 et seq.)
and Title 2, California Administrative Code (Section 18700
et seq.) and the Education Code (Section 1174) provide the
needed legal control of board member actions, and

WHEREAS, the California "Political Reform Act of 1974" is an
infringement on the rights of private citizens and their
immediate families and is an invasion of personal privacy
of board members and their immediate families, and

WHEREAS, this act can require personal expense for attorney and
accountant fees by board members holding elective board
positions, and

WHEREAS, compliance with the requirements of this Code will encourage
private investigative snooping by persons bent on personal
vendettas, and

WHEREAS, compliance with the requirements of the California "Political
Reform Act of 1974" by boards will result in the resignation
of untold numbers of board members throughout the state, and

WHEREAS, school districts and communities of the State need citizens
with financial solvency, business acumen, and personal
stability to serve as board members.

The image appears to be almost entirely blank or white, with only two small circular marks or symbols visible near the top of the page. There is no readable text content to transcribe from this document image.

IT IS HEREBY RESOLVED by the Board of Trustees of the College
School District to publicly state its opposition to the
inclusion of elected public school board members within the
requirements of the California "Political Reform Act of 1974"
and it is resolved to urge its legislators to take the
necessary action to bring about the changes needed to exclude
school board members from the requirements of the California
"Political Reform Act of 1974" and it is resolved to
publicize this resolution.

AYES: Beattie, Davis, Pratt, Turnbull

NOES: None

ABSENT: Lehman


I hereby certify that the foregoing is a true and correct
transcript duly adopted and affirmed by a formal vote of
the members of the Governing Board of the College School
District at a duly constituted regular meeting of the Board
which was open to the public on September 20, 1976,
as it appears upon the minutes of the said meeting of the
said Governing Board.

_________________________
Robert W. Parsons
Superintendent and Secretary to the Board

The image appears to be almost entirely blank or white, with only a few small marks visible at the top (what appear to be two small circular shapes and some scattered dots on the right side). There is no clearly legible text content to transcribe from this document image.
